elements.sent-2411-001: Graph anomaly detection (GAD) is a critical task in graph machine learning, with the primary objective of identifying anomalous nodes that deviate significantly from the majority.
elements.sent-2411-002: This task is widely applied in various real-world scenarios, including fraud detection and social network analysis.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, existing GAD methods still face two major challenges: (1) They are often limited to detecting anomalies in single-type interaction graphs and struggle with multiple interaction types in multiplex heterogeneous graphs; (2) In unsupervised scenarios, selecting appropriate anomaly score thresholds remains a significant challenge for accurate anomaly detection.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address the above challenges, we propose a novel Unsupervised Multiplex Graph Anomaly Detection method, named \model.
elements.sent-2411-005: We first learn multi-relational correlations among nodes in multiplex heterogeneous graphs and capture anomaly information during node attribute and structure reconstruction through graph-masked autoencoder (GMAE).
elements.sent-2411-006: Then, to further weaken the influence of noise and redundant information on abnormal information extraction, we generate attribute-level and subgraph-level augmented-view graphs respectively, and perform attribute and structure reconstruction through GMAE.
elements.sent-2411-007: Finally, We learn to optimize node attributes and structural features through contrastive learning between original-view and augmented-view graphs to improve the model's ability to capture anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-008: Meanwhile, we also propose a new anomaly score threshold selection strategy, which allows the model to be independent of the ground truth in real unsupervised scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-009: Extensive experiments on four datasets show that our \model significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving average improvements of 13.48\% in AUC and 11.68\% in Macro-F1 across all datasets.
elements.sent-2411-010: The source code of our model is available at \url{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/UMGAD-1F3B .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Anomaly detection~\cite{ding2021inductive,ma2021comprehensive , aimed at identifying entities that deviate significantly from normal conditions, is extensively utilized across various applications, for example, fraudulent user detection in financial networks~\cite{huang2022dgraph,dou2020enhancing,liu2021intention,liu2021pick,chen2022antibenford , anomalous behavior detection in social networks~\cite{yang2019mining,cheng2021causal,min2022divide , malicious comments in review networks~\cite{tang2024dualgad , review-scrubbing buffs in e-commerce networks~\cite{zhang2024dig , and so on.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Unlike time series anomaly detection, GAD presents greater challenges due to the inherent complexity of graph data~\cite{yang2023ahead,gao2023addressing , which typically encompasses node attributes and structural features~\cite{tang2022rethinking .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This complexity renders the task of capturing anomalies within graphs particularly formidable.
elements.sent-2411-014: Due to the high cost of acquiring real anomaly data, a large number of existing GAD methods are performed in an unsupervised manner~\cite{xie2023unsupervised,xu2024unsupervised,zhang2023graph,wang2023unsupervised , to detect instances that deviate significantly from the majority of the data.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Various methods have been proposed to solve the unsupervised graph anomaly detection (UGAD) problem.
elements.sent-2411-016: They can be broadly categorized into the following types, \ie, traditional methods, message passing-improved (MPI) methods, contrastive learning (CL)-based methods, and graph autoencoder (GAE)-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-017: Early traditional UGAD methods~\cite{li2017radar are usually based on machine learning algorithms to encode graph information and detect anomalies, or utilize residual information to capture anomalous features.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Message passing-improved methods~\cite{luo2022comga,qiao2024truncated learn anomalous node features by improving the message-passing mechanism of GNNs.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Recently, with the rapid development of graph neural networks (GNNs), more and more CL-based and GAE-based methods have emerged.
elements.sent-2411-020: For example, GRADATE~\cite{duan2023graph performs anomaly detection through a multi-scale contrastive learning network with augmented views, and VGOD~\cite{huang2023unsupervised combines variance-based and graph reconstruction-based models through contrastive learning to detect anomalies.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: ADA-GAD~\cite{he2024ada builds a denoised graph first to train the graph encoder and then trains the graph decoder on the original graph for reconstructing node attributes and structure.. Challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Existing UGAD approaches, CL-based and GAE-based methods, have achieved promising results.
elements.sent-2411-023: Nevertheless, the UGAD task still faces the following two major challenges: (1) Most existing methods focus solely on single-layer heterogeneous graphs, while real-world graphs are often multiplex.
elements.sent-2411-024: Real-world graph data typically includes multiple types of interactions, such as clicking, adding, and buying between users and items in e-commerce networks, or different comments and rating scores in review networks.
elements.sent-2411-025: These interactions result in complex structures, known as multiplex heterogeneous graphs, which have increasingly attracted researchers' attention.
elements.sent-2411-026: Due to the complexity and diversity of interactions within these networks, the GAD task becomes more challenging, complicating the design of effective anomaly detection algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-027: Existing models typically employ two approaches to set the anomaly score threshold: first, by selecting the threshold based on the known number of anomalies when ground truth data is available; second, by choosing the threshold that yields the best model performance.
elements.sent-2411-028: However, these methods are not suitable when the number of anomalies and their labels are unknown, making it unreasonable to rely on these approaches for threshold selection in the unsupervised scenario.. Presented Work.
elements.sent-2411-029: Recognizing the above challenges, we focus on exploring the important impact of different interactive relations on node representation learning and extracting anomaly information through graph reconstructions for multiplex heterogeneous graphs, while finding appropriate anomaly score thresholds in the real unsupervised scenario to improve the anomaly detection performance of the model.
elements.sent-2411-030: To this end, this paper proposes a novel unsupervised multiplex graph anomaly detection method, named \model.
elements.sent-2411-031: We first learn multi-relational correlations among nodes in multiplex heterogeneous graphs and capture anomaly information during node attribute and structure reconstruction through GMAE.
elements.sent-2411-032: This work makes the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-033: We propose a novel unsupervised multiplex graph anomaly detection method that emphasizes the importance of constructing and exploiting different interactions between nodes and addresses the problem of unsupervised anomaly detection in multiplex heterogeneous graphs.
elements.sent-2411-034: We design an effective method for selecting appropriate anomaly score thresholds in the real unsupervised scenario without involving the ground truth by performing anomaly information extraction on both the original- and the augmented-view graphs.
elements.sent-2411-035: We conduct extensive experiments on two datasets with injected anomalies and two with real anomalies to demonstrate the superiority of our proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-036: Experiment results show that \model achieves 13.48\%, and 11.68\% average improvement in terms of AUC and macro-F1 across four datasets compared to SOTA baselines.
elements.sent-2411-037: UGAD has attracted increasing attention in recent years, many researchers focus on how to use self-supervised signals to improve the model's ability to detect anomalies in unsupervised scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-038: Early traditional UGAD methods, such as Radar~\cite{li2017radar and ResGCN~\cite{pei2022resgcn , which capture anomalous features by describing the residuals of attribute information and their consistency with network information, are concise and effective.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: With the rapid development of GNNs, it has also been naturally introduced into the field of anomaly detection, and recent researchers have been trying to develop UGAD models based on GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-040: We broadly categorize GNN-based models into three types: MPI methods, CL-based methods, and GAE-based methods.. MPI methods.
elements.sent-2411-041: It is well known that most of the GNN-based methods exploit the message passing mechanism~\cite{wang2021modeling of GNN to learn node attributes by aggregating the neighborhood information of nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-042: However, due to the feature inconsistency between normal and anomalous nodes, the original message passing mechanism may impair the model's ability to extract the anomalous information.
elements.sent-2411-043: Luo et al.~\cite{luo2022comga improve the message passing mechanism from the perspectives of community segmentation and community structure learning and obtain node attributes by introducing the community structure.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Qiao et al.~\cite{qiao2024truncated propose a truncated affinity maximization method named TAM, which maximizes the local affinity of a node with its neighbor nodes and performs node characterization on the truncated graph instead of the original graph.}
elements.sent-2411-045: As a common method for mining self-supervised signals~\cite{liu2024towards,kong2024federated,liu2024bourne , contrastive learning is also introduced to the UGAD task~\cite{wang2023cross to capture feature inconsistencies between normal and anomalous nodes.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: CoLA~\cite{liu2021anomaly is a novel contrastive instance pair sampling method that leverages local information in network data to learn information embedding from high-dimensional attributes and local structures.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Zhang et al.~\cite{zhang2022reconstruction propose a self-supervised learning framework called Sub-CR that jointly optimizes two modules, multi-view contrastive-based learning, and attribute-based reconstruction, to more accurately detect anomalies on attribute networks.}
elements.sent-2411-048: In addition to focusing on node attribute learning and attribute reconstruction, contrastive learning can also be extended to the subgraph level to capture complex structural anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-049: GRADATE~\cite{duan2023graph , a multi-view and multi-scale contrastive learning framework including node-subgraph contrastive learning and subgraph-subgraph contrastive learning, can better capture different features between normal and abnormal substructures, which helps to identify complex structural anomalies.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Huang et al.~\cite{huang2023unsupervised combine variance-based models and attribute reconstruction models to detect outliers in a balanced way.}
elements.sent-2411-051: GAE-based methods: GAE-based methods~\cite{li2019specae are one of the mainstay methods to detect anomalies by reconstructing the node attributes and structural features in the graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Ding et al.~\cite{ding2019deep use GCN to model graph topology and node attributes to learn node embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Roy et al.~\cite{roy2024gad incorporate neighborhood reconstruction in their proposed GAD-NR, aiming to reconstruct the entire neighborhood of a node based on the corresponding node representation (including local structure, self-properties, and neighborhood properties).}
elements.sent-2411-054: ADA-GAD~\cite{he2024ada introduces a two-stage anomaly denoising autoencoder framework, where the first stage trains the GAE on the generated denoised graphs, and the second stage retains only the encoder and retrains the encoder on the original graphs while introducing regularization of the distribution of node anomalies to prevent anomalous overfitting.}

elements.sent-2411-059: In this section, we propose a novel unsupervised multiplex graph anomaly detection method named \model depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:framework , which mainly includes three key components: (1) Original-view Graph Reconstruction, (2) Augmented-view Graph Reconstruction and (3) Dual-view Contrastive Learning, to capture the anomalous information on both original and augmented graphs, and fuse the attribute and structural information of the nodes learned from the two views by contrastive learning to improve the model anomaly detection performance.}

elements.sent-2411-103: Putting it all together, we have the overall loss function in the training stage is as follows: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{O} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{A\_Aug} + \mu \mathcal{L}_{S\_Aug} + \Theta \mathcal{L}_{CL},\end{equation} where $\lambda$, $\mu$, and $\Theta$ are hyperparameters that measure the importance of different augmented views and contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-104: Based on the final training loss $\mathcal{L}$, we train several autoencoder modules, sort the nodes based on their anomaly scores, $S(i)$, and treat the nodes with higher $S(i)$ as anomalous nodes.
elements.sent-2411-105: For both original-view and augmented-view graphs, we use the following formula to calculate and finally take the arithmetic average of multiple views as the anomaly score of the node $v_i$: \begin{equation}S(i)_{O/A\_Aug/S\_Aug} = \varepsilon \cdot \frac{1}{R}\sum\limits_{r = 1}^R {||\tilde a(i)_{O/A\_Aug/S\_Aug}^r - a(i)^r|{|_1}} \notag + (1 - \varepsilon ) \cdot ||{\tilde{x}_{O/A\_Aug/S\_Aug}(i)} - {x(i)}|{|_2},\end{equation} where $S(i)_{O/A\_Aug/S\_Aug}$ denotes the score in specific graph, $|| \cdot |{|_1}$ denotes the L1-norm of the vector, $|| \cdot |{|_2}$ denotes the Euclidean norm of a vector, $\tilde{a (i)^r$ and $a(i)^r$ represent the $i$-th row of the specific reconstructed structure matrix $\tilde{\mathbf{A _{O/A\_Aug/S\_Aug ^r$ and original structure matrix $\mathbf{A ^r$, respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Similarly, $\tilde{x (i)$ and $x(i)$ are the reconstructed attribute vector and the original attribute vector of the node $v_i$, respectively.}

elements.subsection-2411-006: Anomaly Score Threshold Selection for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection (RQ1)
elements.sent-2411-123: For the unsupervised graph anomaly detection task, since the nodes' label (normal or abnormal) is unknown, a common approach is to compute the anomaly score for each node and rank them in order of the anomaly score from high to low.
elements.sent-2411-124: Nodes with high anomaly scores are considered anomalous nodes.
elements.sent-2411-125: However, determining a more accurate anomaly score threshold has always been a difficult problem in unsupervised graph anomaly detection.
elements.sent-2411-126: A good unsupervised anomaly detection model with its calculated anomaly scores for normal and abnormal nodes should be easy to distinguish, and the thresholds for dividing the abnormal nodes from the normal nodes are also relatively easy to set.
elements.sent-2411-127: To find a suitable anomaly score threshold, we visualize the ranked node anomaly scores of different methods and the corresponding threshold selection strategy, \ie, we select the anomaly score value of the convergence tangent point in the anomaly score curve as the anomaly score threshold.
elements.sent-2411-128: The results on two datasets with real anomalies are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:threshold , and the results on two datasets with injected anomalies are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:threshold2 in the Supplement.}}
elements.sent-2411-129: In Fig.~\ref{fig:threshold , we show the trend of the ranked node anomaly scores for the best-performing baselines, TAM, ADA-GAD, GADAM, and AnomMAN, as well as for our \model, on Amazon and YelpChi respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-130: As can be seen in Fig.~\ref{fig:threshold , compared with the other four best-performing baselines, the curve of our \model can converge quickly (become stable) to a position closer to the actual number of anomalies in the datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-131: It allows \model to better distinguish the anomaly scores of anomalous nodes from those of normal nodes, and thus be able to identify anomalies more accurately.
elements.sent-2411-132: A good unsupervised anomaly detection model should be able to clearly distinguish the anomaly scores of abnormal nodes from those of normal nodes.
elements.sent-2411-133: Therefore, we use the anomaly score at the convergence of the curve as the anomaly score threshold.
elements.sent-2411-134: After introducing the ground truth we find that the number of anomalous nodes corresponding to the threshold selected by our \model is very close to the real number of anomalies, which proves that our method can effectively alleviate the difficult problem of selecting anomaly score thresholds in the UGAD problem.
elements.sent-2411-135: The experimental results in Retail and Alibaba dataset are shown in the Supplement.
elements.subsection-2411-007: Performance Comparison in the Real Unsupervised Scenario (RQ2)
elements.sent-2411-136: Next, we evaluate the performance of our \model and all baselines in real unsupervised scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-137: That is, we use the convergence point of the ranked anomaly score curve as the anomaly score threshold for each method.
elements.sent-2411-138: This threshold selection method does not require the introduction of any ground-truth information and is suitable for real unsupervised anomaly detection scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-139: The experimental results are shown in Table~\ref{tab:baselines_threshold .}
elements.sent-2411-140: The best results are highlighted in bold, and the second-best results are underlined.
elements.sent-2411-141: As we can see, our \model achieves the optimal performance, significantly outperforming all baselines in both AUC and macro-F1 metrics on four datasets, with specific emphasis on the improvement relative to the SOTA methods.
elements.sent-2411-142: We can draw the following conclusions: first, according to our proposed threshold selection strategy and the node anomaly score curves in Fig.~\ref{fig:threshold , the number of detected anomalies corresponding to our selected thresholds is the closest to the real situation compared to other methods; second, our UMGAD method considers the multi-relational correlation between nodes, and mitigates the effects of noise and redundant information by introducing enhanced views that allow the model to capture the anomaly information more efficiently so that the anomaly scores of anomalous nodes can be better distinguished from those of normal nodes.}

elements.sent-2411-149: Meanwhile, based on the experimental results of w/o NA and w/o SA, we can find that the augmented-view GMAE can capture anomalous information, which is difficult for original-view GMAE, because node attributes and structural features in the augmented graph have changed, mitigating the problem that GNN-based autoencoders are prone to fall into the homogeneity trap.

elements.sent-2411-152: To investigate how these two main factors change the performance of our model, we analyze the trend of different values on four datasets.
elements.sent-2411-153: The values of subgraph size $|\mathcal{V _m|$ (\ie, the number of nodes in the subgraph) are selected in \{4, 8, 12, 16\}, masking ratio $r_m$ range in \{20\%, 40\%, 60\%, 80\%\}.}

elements.sent-2411-155: While Retail and Alibaba enjoy higher performance with 20\% masking.

elements.sent-2411-157: In this work, we propose a model called \model for unsupervised multiplex graph anomaly detection tasks.
elements.sent-2411-158: Specifically, we reconstruct node attributes and structures and learn multi-relational correlations among nodes from two views: the original view and the augmented view.
elements.sent-2411-159: Extensive experiments on two datasets with injected anomalies and two real-world datasets with real anomalies show that \model outperforms SOTA methods in real unsupervised scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-160: Further ablation studies validate the effectiveness of each component of our model.
elements.sent-2411-161: Three datasets are adopted for evaluation:.
elements.sent-2411-163: This is a benchmark dataset collected from the Retail\_rocket recommendation system.
elements.sent-2411-164: In this dataset, interactive relations between users and items include page views (View), add-to-cart (Cart), and transactions (Buy).
elements.sent-2411-165: This dataset is collected from Alibaba, one of the largest e-commerce platforms in China.
elements.sent-2411-166: It contains three interactive relations between users and items, \ie, page view (View), add to cart (Cart), and purchase (Buy).
elements.sent-2411-167: This dataset is collected from Amazon, it contains three interactive relations among users: U-P-U links users reviewing at least one product, U-S-U links users having at least one identical star rating within a week, U-V-U links linguistically similar users.
elements.sent-2411-168: This dataset is collected from Yelp, it contains three interactive relations among users: R-U-R connects the reviews posted by the same user, R-S-R connects reviews that have the same identical star rating, R-T-R connects reviews that posted in the same term.
elements.sent-2411-169: We compare our \model with the following 20 baselines, which can be deeply divided into three categories: Traditional GAD Method:.
elements.sent-2411-170: Radar~\cite{li2017radar: It is a learning framework for characterizing the residuals of attribute information in anomaly detection and its consistency with network information.}
elements.sent-2411-172: ComGA~\cite{luo2022comga: It is a novel community-aware attribute graph anomaly detection framework that contains a customized deep graph convolutional network which is capable of improving the information dissemination mechanism of GNNs from the perspectives of community segmentation and community structure and feature learning.}
elements.sent-2411-173: TAM~\cite{qiao2024truncated: It learns custom node embeddings for our anomaly measure by maximizing the local affinity of nodes with their neighbors.}
elements.sent-2411-174: TAM is optimized on a truncated graph rather than the original graph, where non-homogeneous edges are iteratively removed to mitigate non-homogeneous edge (\ie., edges connecting anomalous nodes to normal nodes) bias.
elements.sent-2411-176: CoLA~\cite{liu2021anomaly: It proposes a well-designed GNN-based contrastive learning model to learn the information embedding from high-dimensional attributes and local structures, which makes full use of the local information in the network data by designing a novel contrastive instance pair sampling method.}
elements.sent-2411-177: ANEMONE~\cite{jin2021anemone: It first utilizes a GNN backbone encoder with a multi-scale contrastive learning objective to capture the pattern distribution of graphs by simultaneously learning the consistency between segment and context-level instances; then a statistical anomaly estimator is employed to assess the anomalies of each node from multiple perspectives based on the degree of consistency.}
elements.sent-2411-178: Sub-CR~\cite{zhang2022reconstruction: It is a self-supervised learning framework that jointly optimizes two modules, multi-view comparison-based learning as well as attribute-based reconstruction, to more accurately detect anomalies on attribute networks.}
elements.sent-2411-179: ARISE~\cite{duan2023arise: It is a novel framework for graph anomaly detection based on substructure-aware attribute networks.ARISE differs from previous algorithms by focusing on learning substructures in graphs to recognize anomalies.}
elements.sent-2411-180: SL-GAD~\cite{zheng2021generative: It constructs different contextual views based on the target nodes and uses two modules, Generative Attribute Regression and Multi-View Comparative Learning, for anomaly detection.}
elements.sent-2411-181: The Generative Attribute Regression module allows for capturing attribute anomalies, while the Multi-View Comparative Learning module can utilize richer structural information from multiple views to capture structural anomalies as well as mixed structural and attribute anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-182: PREM~\cite{pan2023prem: It consists of two modules - a preprocessing module and an isochronous bit matching module - designed to eliminate the need for message-passing propagation during training and employs a simple contrastive loss, which reduces training time and memory usage.}
elements.sent-2411-183: GCCAD~\cite{chen2022gccad: It proposes a pre-training strategy to solve the label scarcity problem in anomaly detection.}
elements.sent-2411-184: Inspired by the idea of context-aware comparison targeting, GCCAD suggests constructing pseudo anomalies by corrupting the original graph.
elements.sent-2411-185: GRADATE~\cite{duan2023graph: It is a multi-view multi-scale contrastive learning framework including node-subgraph contrastive learning and subgraph-subgraph contrastive learning, which can better capture the different features between normal and abnormal substructures and help to recognize complex structural anomalies.}
elements.sent-2411-186: VGOD~\cite{huang2023unsupervised: It is a variance-based graph outlier detection method that combines a variance-based model and an attribute reconstruction model to detect outliers in a balanced way.}
elements.sent-2411-188: GCNAE~\cite{kipf2016variational: It is an unsupervised learning framework for graph-structured data based on a variational graph autoencoder, which uses GCN as an encoder, simple vector inner product as a decoder, and latent variables to learn interpretable potential representational embeddings of undirected graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-189: DOMINANT~\cite{ding2019deep: It learns node embeddings by modeling graph topology and node attributes using GCN and uses the learned node embeddings to reconstruct the original data via a deep autoencoder, with good results.}
elements.sent-2411-190: AnomalyDAE~\cite{fan2020anomalydae: It is a deep joint representation learning framework for anomaly detection via dual self-encoders that captures complex interactions between network structure and node attributes to obtain high-quality network embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-191: AdONE~\cite{bandyopadhyay2020outlier: It is a deep unsupervised autoencoder-based approach and incorporates adversarial learning to learn the structure of the network and attribute-based embedding separately in a coupled manner to minimize the impact of outliers.}
elements.sent-2411-192: GAD-NR~\cite{roy2024gad: It combines neighborhood reconstruction for graph anomaly detection, aiming to reconstruct the entire neighborhood of a node based on the corresponding node representation, including the local structure, self-attributes, and neighbor attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-193: ADA-GAD~\cite{he2024ada: It is a novel two-stage anomaly denoising autoencoder framework.}
elements.sent-2411-194: In the first stage, the denoising method generates graphs with reduced anomaly levels and trains a graph autoencoder on them.
elements.sent-2411-195: In the second stage, the decoder is discarded while the encoder is retained, and a new decoder is trained for detection on the original graph.
elements.sent-2411-196: Additionally, ADA-GAD introduces node anomaly distribution regularization to further prevent anomaly overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-197: GADAM~\cite{chen2024boosting: It is a novel Local Inconsistency Mining (LIM) approach that resolves the conflict between LIM and GNN messaging and enhances anomaly detection through an improved message passing mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-198: AnomMAN~\cite{chen2023anomman: It proposes a GCN-based framework for detecting anomalies on multi-view attribute networks called AnomMAN that considers attributes and various interactions on multi-view attribute networks and uses an attention mechanism to fuse the importance of all views.}
elements.sent-2411-199: The proposed \model consists of three critical components: original-view graph reconstruction, augmented-view graph reconstruction, and dual-view contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-200: In original-view graph reconstruction, the time complexity of GMAE is $O(|\mathcal{V | \times L \times f)$, and the time complexity of attention aggregation operation is $O(|\mathcal{V | \times d_h \times f \times R$, where $|\mathcal{V |$ denotes the number of nodes, and $L$ represents the number of Simplified GCN layers, $d_h$ and $f$ denote the dimension of latent vectors and the size of node attribute, $R$ is the number of relational subgraphs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-201: The overall time complexity of original-view graph reconstruction is $O(|\mathcal{V | \times f \times (L + d_h \times R)$.}
elements.sent-2411-202: For augmented-view graph reconstruction, the overall time complexity is $O(|\mathcal{V | \times f \times (L + d_h \times R)$.}
elements.sent-2411-203: The time complexity for dual-view contrastive learning is $O(|\mathcal{V |\times f^2 + |\mathcal{V |\times f)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-204: Hence, the entire time complexity of the proposed \model is $O(|\mathcal{V | \times f \times (L + d_h \times R + f + 1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-205: Additionally, original-view graph reconstruction and augmented-view graph reconstruction can also be accelerated in a parallel manner.

elements.subsubsection-2411-011: Performance Comparison in the Ground Truth Leakage Scenario (RQ5)
elements.sent-2411-208: Experimental results are reported in Table~\ref{tab:baselines .}
elements.sent-2411-209: From Table~\ref{tab:baselines , we can see that even with the introduction of ground truth, our model still achieves the best performance, which proves that \model can capture anomaly information and identify anomalous nodes more effectively.}
elements.sent-2411-210: For the traditional GAD method, Radar uses residual networks to learn node attributes and capture anomalies simply and effectively, it is not as effective in capturing anomalous features when facing complex network structures.
elements.sent-2411-211: For the specifically designed for improving message passing mechanism (Specific for short) GAD methods, TAM performs better than ComGA since it learns custom node embeddings for our anomaly measure by maximizing the local affinity of nodes with their neighbors, and attenuates the effect of non-homogeneity of connected edges between abnormal and normal nodes.
elements.sent-2411-212: For the CL-based GAD methods, GRADATE, VGOD, and ARISE are the three most effective of all methods.
elements.sent-2411-213: All three methods consider the importance of anomalous structural subgraphs to the GAD problem and learn subgraph features through subgraph sampling strategies to capture structural anomalies that are not easily detected by other methods.
elements.sent-2411-214: For the GAE-based GAD methods, most of them are working on how to learn better features of anomalous nodes for better detection of anomalous nodes during attribute and structural feature reconstruction, the four most effective and representative of these are GADAM, AnomMAN, GAD-NR, and ADA-GAD.
elements.sent-2411-215: Among them, ADA-GAD learns anomalous node features by graph augmentation, GAD-NR incorporates neighborhood reconstruction for the GAD task, and GADAM utilizes improved GNN message passing to enhance anomaly detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-216: However, these methods cannot solve the GAD problem on multiplex heterogeneous graphs, and thus perform slightly worse than our \model.
elements.sent-2411-217: In addition, AnomMAN distinguishes itself from other GAE models in that it is oriented to the multiplex heterogeneous graphs constructed from ordinary heterogeneous graphs, which is similar to the object of study of our \model.
elements.sent-2411-218: The performance of AnomMAN is limited since it does not capture subgraph structural anomalies well.
elements.subsubsection-2411-012: The effect of weights $\alpha$ and $\beta$ that balance the importance of attribute and structure reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-219: In this subsection, we perform a sensitivity analysis on $\alpha$ and $\beta$, two important hyperparameters for balancing attribute and structure reconstructions in the original view and subgraph-level augmented view, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-220: Experimental results are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:alpha_beta .}
elements.sent-2411-221: We can observe that: (1) the performance of \model decreases rapidly when $\alpha$ and $\beta$ take smaller $(<0.2)$ and larger $(>0.8)$ values, which emphasizes the importance of balancing feature and structure reconstructions, and smaller or larger values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ break the balance of the two reconstructions, resulting in the failure to capture more adequate anomaly information and affecting the model performance.
elements.sent-2411-222: However, existing GAD methods still face two major challenges: (1) They are often limited to detecting anomalies in single-type interaction graphs and struggle with multiple interaction types in multiplex heterogeneous graphs.
elements.sent-2411-223: Then, to further extract abnormal information, we generate attribute-level and subgraph-level augmented-view graphs respectively, and perform attribute and structure reconstruction through GMAE.
elements.sent-2411-224: Meanwhile, we also propose a new anomaly score threshold selection strategy, which allows the model to be independent of ground truth information in real unsupervised scenarios.

elements.sent-2411-231: Nevertheless, the UGAD task still faces the following two major challenges: (1) Most existing methods focus solely on non-multiplex heterogeneous graphs, while real-world graphs are often multiplex that typically include multiple types of interactions.
elements.sent-2411-232: For example, there exists viewing, carting, and buying relations between users and items in e-commerce networks, or users' different comments and rating scores on items in review networks.
elements.sent-2411-233: These interactions result in complex structures, known as multiplex heterogeneous graphs.
elements.sent-2411-234: Anomaly detection in multiplex heterogeneous graphs is challenging because the interacting multiple relations are extremely complex and different types of relations have different effects on anomaly detection.
elements.sent-2411-235: Existing models typically employ two approaches to select the anomaly score threshold: 1) by selecting the threshold based on the known number of anomalies when ground truth information in the test set is available, and 2) by choosing the threshold that yields the best model performance.
elements.sent-2411-236: However, these methods are not suitable when the number of anomalies and their labels are unknown, making it unreasonable to rely on these approaches for threshold selection in the real unsupervised scenario with no ground truth information from the test set.. elements.subsection-2411-014: Unsupervised Anomaly Score Threshold Selection Strategy
elements.sent-2411-317: However, determining a more accurate anomaly score threshold has always been a difficult problem in UGAD.
elements.sent-2411-318: To determine an anomaly score threshold for unsupervised graph anomaly detection, we propose a strategy that combines moving average smoothing and inflection point detection.
elements.sent-2411-319: This approach works directly on the ordered anomaly score sequence, without relying on ground truth information from the test set (e.g., the number of anomalous nodes), making it suitable for real unsupervised scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-320: Given the learned anomaly score set for $|\mathcal{V |$ nodes, we first sort the scores in descending order to form $S = \{s(1), s(2), \dots, s(|\mathcal{V |) \} $, where $s(1) \geq s(2) \geq \dots \geq s(|\mathcal{V |)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-321: The sorted sequence reflects the relative magnitude of anomaly scores, with higher scores more likely corresponding to anomalous nodes.
elements.sent-2411-322: To mitigate the effects of noise and ensure a smooth transition in the sequence, we apply a moving average smoothing technique.
elements.sent-2411-323: For a given sliding window size $w$, the smoothed score sequence $\bar{S}$ is computed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq20} \bar{s}(i) = \frac{1}{w} \sum_{j=i}^{i+w-1} s(j), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, |\mathcal{V}|-w+1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-324: The choice of $w$ balances noise reduction and the preservation of meaningful variations in the scores.
elements.sent-2411-325: A practical guideline is to set $w = \max(\lfloor 0.0001|\mathcal{V | \rfloor, 5)$, which works well across different data scales.}
elements.sent-2411-326: After smoothing, we calculate the first-order and second-order differences of the sequence to analyze its structural changes.
elements.sent-2411-327: The first-order difference is: \begin{equation}\label{eq21} \Delta_1(i) = \bar{s}(i) - \bar{s}(i+1), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, |\mathcal{V}|-w,\end{equation} while the second-order difference is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq22} \Delta_2(i) = \Delta_1(i) - \Delta_1(i+1), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, |\mathcal{V}|-w-1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-328: Here, $\Delta_1(i)$ captures the local decrease in scores, and $\Delta_2(i)$ identifies changes in the rate of decrease.
elements.sent-2411-329: The second-order difference captures the inflection point where the decline in anomaly scores transitions from steep (anomalous nodes) to stable (normal nodes).
elements.sent-2411-330: The inflection point $k$ is identified as the index $i$ where the magnitude of $\Delta_2(i)$ reaches its maximum: \begin{equation}\label{eq23} \mathcal{T} = \arg\max_i |\Delta_2(i)|, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, |\mathcal{V}|-w-1,\end{equation} which corresponds to the region where the anomaly score sequence transitions from a steep decline to a plateau.
elements.sent-2411-331: The anomaly score threshold $s(\mathcal{T )$ is then defined as the smoothed score at the inflection point $\mathcal{T}$.}

elements.sent-2411-333: Therefore, nodes with scores $s(i) \geq s(\mathcal{T )$ are considered anomalous, while those with scores $s(i) < s(\mathcal{T )$ are deemed normal.}}
elements.sent-2411-334: We believe that a good anomaly detection model should be able to clearly distinguish between anomalous and normal nodes based on anomaly scores.

elements.sent-2411-342: Specifically, for synthetic anomalies, we follow the approach from [8] to introduce both structural and attribute anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-343: When injecting structural anomalies, we specify that a clique of size $m$ is introduced, \ie, if $m$ nodes are randomly selected from the network and then these nodes are made fully connected, all $m$ nodes in the clique are considered as anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-344: We iteratively repeat this process until $n$ factions are generated and the total number of structural anomalies is $m \times n$.
elements.sent-2411-345: Correspondingly, when injecting attribute anomalies, we first randomly select additional $m \times n$ nodes as attribute perturbation candidates.
elements.sent-2411-346: For each selected node $i$, we randomly select another $k$ nodes from the data and select the node $j$ whose attribute among the $k$ nodes deviates most from node $i$ by maximizing the Euclidean distance ${||x_i-x_j|| ^2$ .}
elements.sent-2411-347: Then, we change the attribute $x_i$ of node $i$ to $x_j$.
elements.sent-2411-348: Additionally, for datasets containing real anomalies, we directly use the publicly available datasets from [9].
elements.sent-2411-349: We mainly compare five categories of methods on the unsupervised GAD task including the traditional method Radar~\cite{li2017radar .. Traditional GAD Method:.}
elements.sent-2411-350: ComGA~\cite{luo2022comga: It is a community-aware anomaly detection framework that enhances GNNs through community-based segmentation and feature learning.}
elements.sent-2411-351: RAND~\cite{bei2023reinforcement: It integrates reinforced neighborhood selection and improves the message-passing mechanism for unsupervised graph anomaly detection.}
elements.sent-2411-352: TAM~\cite{qiao2024truncated: It learns node embeddings for anomaly detection by maximizing local affinity and removing non-homogeneous edges to reduce bias.}
elements.sent-2411-353: CoLA~\cite{liu2021anomaly: It proposes a GNN-based contrastive model that captures high-dimensional attributes and local structures using a novel sampling method.}
elements.sent-2411-354: ANEMONE~\cite{jin2021anemone: It employs a GNN encoder with multi-scale contrastive learning to capture graph patterns and a statistical estimator to detect node anomalies based on consistency.}
elements.sent-2411-355: Sub-CR~\cite{zhang2022reconstruction: It is a self-supervised framework combining multi-view comparison and attribute reconstruction to detect anomalies on attribute networks.}
elements.sent-2411-356: ARISE~\cite{duan2023arise: It is a graph anomaly detection model that identifies anomalies by learning substructures in attribute networks.}
elements.sent-2411-357: SL-GAD~\cite{zheng2021generative: It uses contextual views with generative attribute regression and multi-view comparative learning to detect attribute, structural, and mixed anomalies.}
elements.sent-2411-358: PREM~\cite{pan2023prem: It includes a preprocessing module and an isochronous bit matching module to eliminate message-passing during training, using a simple contrastive loss to reduce training time and memory usage.}
elements.sent-2411-359: GCCAD~\cite{chen2022gccad: It proposes a pre-training strategy to address label scarcity by generating pseudo anomalies through graph corruption.}
elements.sent-2411-360: GRADATE~\cite{duan2023graph: It is a multi-view, multi-scale contrastive learning framework that captures features to detect complex structural anomalies.}
elements.sent-2411-361: VGOD~\cite{huang2023unsupervised: It is a variance-based graph outlier detection method that combines variance modeling and attribute reconstruction for balanced detection.}
elements.sent-2411-362: GCNAE~\cite{kipf2016variational: It is an unsupervised model using a variational graph autoencoder (VGAE) with GCN encoding and latent variables to learn graph embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-363: DOMINANT~\cite{ding2019deep: It learns node embeddings using GCN to model graph topology and attributes, then reconstructs the original data with a deep autoencoder.}
elements.sent-2411-364: AnomalyDAE~\cite{fan2020anomalydae: It is a joint representation learning model for anomaly detection using dual self-encoders to capture interactions between structure and node attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-365: AdONE~\cite{bandyopadhyay2020outlier: It is an unsupervised AE approach with adversarial learning to separately learn network structure and attribute embeddings, minimizing the impact of outliers.}
elements.sent-2411-366: GAD-NR~\cite{roy2024gad: It combines neighborhood reconstruction for GAD problem, aiming to reconstruct a node's entire neighborhood, including its local structure and attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-367: ADA-GAD~\cite{he2024ada: It is a two-stage anomaly denoising autoencoder framework that reduces anomalies, trains a graph autoencoder, and applies node anomaly distribution regularization to prevent overfitting.}
elements.sent-2411-368: GADAM~\cite{chen2024boosting: It is a local inconsistency mining approach that resolves conflicts with GNN messaging and enhances anomaly detection through improved message passing.}
elements.sent-2411-369: AnomMAN~\cite{chen2023anomman: It is a GCN-based framework for anomaly detection on multi-view attribute networks, using attention to fuse information across views.}
elements.sent-2411-370: DualGAD~\cite{tang2024dualgad: It is a self-supervised model combining generative and contrastive modules with masked subgraph reconstruction for improved node-level anomaly detection.}

elements.sent-2411-490: Retail\_Rocket (Retail for short) contains user-item interactions such as page views (View), add-to-cart (Cart), and transactions (Buy).
elements.sent-2411-491: Alibaba includes three types of user-item interactions like Retail: View, Cart, and Buy.
elements.sent-2411-492: Amazon contains three types of user interactions: U-P-U links users who have reviewed at least one common product, U-S-U links users with identical star ratings within a week, and U-V-U links linguistically similar users.
elements.sent-2411-493: YelpChi contains three interactive relations among users: R-U-R connects the reviews posted by the same user, R-S-R connects reviews that have the same identical star rating, R-T-R connects reviews that posted in the same term.
elements.sent-2411-494: DGraph-Fin (DG-Fin for short) contains three interactive relations among users: U-C-U connects the emergency contact among users, U-B-U connects the borrowing behavior among users, U-R-U indicates that users follow each other.
elements.sent-2411-495: T-Social contains three interactive relations among users: U-R-U links users who are friends with each other, U-F-U links users having fraud or money laundering behavior, U-G-U links online gambling users.

elements.sent-2411-496: For anomaly injection, we follow the method in~\cite{ding2019interactive to introduce both structural and attribute anomalies.}
elements.sent-2411-497: For structural anomalies, a clique of size $m$ is formed by randomly selecting $m$ nodes and fully connecting them with one or multiple randomly assigned relation types.
elements.sent-2411-498: All $m$ nodes in the clique are considered anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-499: This process is repeated iteratively until $n$ factions are created, yielding $m \times n$ structural anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-500: For attribute anomalies, we randomly select $m \times n$ nodes and for each, identify the most deviant attribute by maximizing the Euclidean distance ${||x_i - x_j|| ^2$ between node $i$ and a randomly selected node $j$.}
elements.sent-2411-501: We then update node $i$’s attribute to match node $j$'s.
elements.sent-2411-502: For datasets with real anomalies, we directly use publicly available datasets.
elements.sent-2411-503: We mainly compare five categories of methods on the unsupervised GAD task.

elements.sent-2411-505: Radar~\cite{li2017radar characterizes the residuals of attribute information and its consistency with network information.}
elements.sent-2411-507: ComGA~\cite{luo2022comga enhances GNNs through community-based segmentation and feature learning.}
elements.sent-2411-508: RAND~\cite{bei2023reinforcement integrates reinforced neighborhood selection and improves the message-passing mechanism for UGAD.}
elements.sent-2411-509: TAM~\cite{qiao2024truncated learns embeddings by maximizing local affinity and removing non-homogeneous edges to reduce bias.}
elements.sent-2411-510: CoLA~\cite{liu2021anomaly proposes a GNN-based contrastive model that captures high-dimensional attributes and local structures.}
elements.sent-2411-511: ANEMONE~\cite{jin2021anemone combines a multi-scale contrastive GNN encoder and a statistical consistency estimator.}
elements.sent-2411-512: Sub-CR~\cite{zhang2022reconstruction combines multi-view comparison and attribute reconstruction to detect anomalies on attribute networks.}
elements.sent-2411-513: ARISE~\cite{duan2023arise identifies anomalies by learning substructures in attribute networks.}
elements.sent-2411-514: SL-GAD~\cite{zheng2021generative uses contextual views with generative attribute regression and multi-view comparative learning.}
elements.sent-2411-515: PREM~\cite{pan2023prem eliminates training-phase message passing through preprocessing and isochronous bit matching.}
elements.sent-2411-516: GCCAD~\cite{chen2022gccad proposes a pre-training strategy that generates pseudo anomalies via graph corruption to face label scarcity.}
elements.sent-2411-517: GRADATE~\cite{duan2023graph is a multi-view, multi-scale contrastive learning model that detects complex structural anomalies.}
elements.sent-2411-518: VGOD~\cite{huang2023unsupervised is a variance-based model that combines variance modeling and attribute reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-519: GCNAE~\cite{kipf2016variational is an unsupervised model using a VGAE with GCN encoding and latent variables to embed graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-520: DOMINANT~\cite{ding2019deep models graph topology and attributes using GCN and reconstructs the original graph with AE.}
elements.sent-2411-521: AnomalyDAE~\cite{fan2020anomalydae uses dual self-encoders to capture interactions between structure and node attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-522: AdONE~\cite{bandyopadhyay2020outlier is an unsupervised AE approach with adversarial learning to separately learn structure and attribute.}
elements.sent-2411-523: GAD-NR~\cite{roy2024gad combines neighborhood reconstruction to reconstruct a node's entire neighborhood.}
elements.sent-2411-524: ADA-GAD~\cite{he2024ada is a two-stage anomaly denoising autoencoder that reduces anomalies, trains a graph autoencoder, and applies node regularization to prevent overfitting.}
elements.sent-2411-525: GADAM~\cite{chen2024boosting is a local inconsistency mining approach that resolves conflicts with GNN messaging.}
elements.sent-2411-526: AnomMAN~\cite{chen2023anomman focuses UGAD on multi-view attribute networks using attention to fuse information across views.}
elements.sent-2411-527: DualGAD~\cite{tang2024dualgad combines generative and contrastive modules with masked subgraph reconstruction for UGAD.}

elements.subsubsection-2411-020: The trade-off between efficiency and accuracy
elements.sent-2411-555: We evaluate the trade-off between efficiency and accuracy for our \model by analyzing its runtime and AUC scores across different variants.
elements.sent-2411-556: Specifically, Att excludes structural reconstruction, retaining only attribute reconstruction in both the original and augmented views of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-557: Str removes attribute reconstruction, focusing solely on structural reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-558: Sub only includes the subgraph reconstruction mechanism in both views.
elements.sent-2411-559: For Att and Str, we inject attribute or structural anomalies, respectively, to simulate scenarios where the anomalies are either attribute- or structure-based.
elements.sent-2411-560: Experimental results, shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:tradeoff , reveal that by selectively pruning the model for different anomaly types, we can enhance efficiency without sacrificing significant performance, adapting to various anomaly scenarios.}

elements.subsection-2411-021: Performance Comparison in the Ground Truth Leakage Scenario (RQ6)
elements.sent-2411-561: For traditional GAD methods, Radar utilizes residual networks to learn node attributes and capture anomalies simply and effectively.
elements.sent-2411-562: Among specifically designed MPI methods, TAM outperforms ComGA and RAND by learning custom node embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-563: AnomMAN focuses on multi-view graphs derived from ordinary heterogeneous graphs, similar to our \model.
elements.sent-2411-564: However, its performance is limited by its inability to effectively capture subgraph structural anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-565: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:time , \model is faster than other baselines regarding single-epoch and total runtime across all datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-566: Significantly, on the large-scale dataset T-Social, \model achieves the shortest epoch and total runtime, highlighting its superior efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-567: Additionally, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:tl , \model demonstrates rapid convergence during training, reaching optimal performance with fewer epochs.}
elements.sent-2411-568: This combination of efficiency and robust anomaly detection performance underscores \model's practicality and scalability, making it a probable choice for large-scale, time-sensitive graph analysis tasks.
elements.sent-2411-569: In this paper, we propose \model, an unsupervised framework for anomaly detection on multiplex heterogeneous graphs.
elements.sent-2411-570: It combines different relation-aware masking mechanisms with graph autoencoders to capture informative signals while reducing redundancy.
elements.sent-2411-571: Attribute and structure reconstruction on both original and augmented views, followed by dual-view contrastive learning, ensures robust anomaly detection.
elements.sent-2411-572: Extensive experiments on datasets with injected and real-world anomalies show that \model outperforms state-of-the-art methods in accuracy and efficiency.
